content.body:
You have an 8-bit grayscale image (256 gray shades) but your printer can only print **1-bit** (ink or no ink). Dithering trades **intensity resolution** for **spatial resolution** ‚Äî instead of one pixel per dot, one pixel becomes a small **pattern of dots**.

Your eyes average nearby dots together. Half black, half white in an area ‚Üí eye perceives it as **medium gray**, even though there's no actual gray dot.

An analog technique used in newspapers. Varying-size filled circles of black ink represent shading ‚Äî bigger circles = darker areas, smaller = lighter. Look at a newspaper photo with a magnifying glass and you'll see it.

**Dithering is the digital version of halftone printing.**

A small grid of threshold values that controls **at what brightness each dot turns on**. Each position has a different trigger point.

The 2√ó2 matrix from the lecture:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
- Position (0,0) threshold **0** ‚Üí turns on first (easiest)
- Position (1,1) threshold **1** ‚Üí turns on second
- Position (0,1) threshold **2** ‚Üí turns on third
- Position (1,0) threshold **3** ‚Üí turns on last (hardest)

The values are arranged in a **scattered pattern** so dots spread out evenly rather than clustering.

content.title: How Dots Fill In (2√ó2 Matrix)
content.body:
The order dots turn on as pixel gets darker:

**Step 1** (value > 0): top-left turns on ‚Üí `‚ñÝ .` / `. .`

**Step 2** (value > 1): bottom-right turns on (diagonal) ‚Üí `‚ñÝ .` / `. ‚ñÝ`

**Step 3** (value > 2): top-right turns on ‚Üí `‚ñÝ ‚ñÝ` / `. ‚ñÝ`

**Step 4** (value > 3): all filled ‚Üí `‚ñÝ ‚ñÝ` / `‚ñÝ ‚ñÝ`

See how dots **spread out evenly** instead of filling left-to-right? That's the whole point of the arrangement.

## Approach 1: Simple Dithering (Each pixel ‚Üí n√ón block)

**Step 1:** Rescale pixel value (0‚Äì255) to range 0 to n¬≤:
$$\text{new\_value} = \lfloor \text{pixel\_value} \div (256 / (n^2 + 1)) \rfloor$$
For 2√ó2: $\text{new\_value} = \lfloor \text{pixel\_value} \div 51.2 \rfloor$

**Step 2:** The new_value = how many dots to turn ON in the block.

**Step 3:** If new_value > matrix_entry at that position ‚Üí dot ON.

**Drawback:** Output image becomes **n¬≤ times larger** (2√ó2 matrix = 4√ó larger, 4√ó4 matrix = 16√ó larger).

content.title: Simple Dithering ‚Äî Worked Examples (2√ó2 Matrix)
content.body:
Matrix: $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, divisor = 51.2

**Pixel = 180:** rescaled = $\lfloor 180/51.2 \rfloor = 3$
- (0,0): 3>0? YES ‚Üí ON
- (0,1): 3>2? YES ‚Üí ON
- (1,0): 3>3? NO ‚Üí OFF
- (1,1): 3>1? YES ‚Üí ON
Result: 3/4 dots ‚Üí `‚ñÝ ‚ñÝ` / `. ‚ñÝ` (fairly dark ‚Äî makes sense for 180/255)

**Pixel = 0:** rescaled=0. 0>nothing ‚Üí all OFF ‚Üí `white`

**Pixel = 60:** rescaled=1. Only 1>0 ‚Üí 1 dot ‚Üí `‚ñÝ .` / `. .`

**Pixel = 130:** rescaled=2. 2>0 and 2>1 ‚Üí 2 dots ‚Üí `‚ñÝ .` / `. ‚ñÝ`

**Pixel = 255:** rescaled=4. 4>everything ‚Üí all 4 ON ‚Üí `black`

The smarter version. Each pixel stays as **one pixel** ‚Äî output image is the same size.

**How:** For pixel at position (row i, col j):
1. Find matrix position: row = $i \bmod n$, col = $j \bmod n$ (matrix **tiles** across the image)
2. Rescale: $\lfloor \text{pixel\_value} \div 64 \rfloor$ (for 2√ó2, range 0‚Äì3)
3. If rescaled > threshold ‚Üí **black (1)**, else ‚Üí **white (0)**

**Dithering also works for color!** A 24-bit color image reduced to 5 bits looks blocky with visible color banding. With dithering, the dot patterns simulate the in-between colors ‚Äî the result looks much smoother.

**Simple dithering:** replaces each pixel with an n√ón block ‚Üí image gets n¬≤ times larger.

**Ordered dithering:** matrix tiles across the image, each pixel stays one pixel ‚Üí same image size.

The comparison: strictly greater than (**>**) the threshold, not ‚â•.

flashcards.a: Trades intensity resolution for spatial resolution. Simulates gray shades on a 1-bit printer using dot patterns. Your eye averages nearby dots ‚Äî half black/half white looks like medium gray.

flashcards.q: What is the connection between dithering and halftone printing?
flashcards.a: Halftone printing is the analog version ‚Äî newspapers use varying-size ink circles. Dithering is the digital equivalent. Both fool the eye into seeing shades using only black and white dots.

flashcards.q: What is the 2√ó2 dither matrix? In what order do dots turn on?
flashcards.a: Matrix: [[0,2],[3,1]]. Dot order: (0,0) with threshold 0 turns on first, then (1,1) threshold 1, then (0,1) threshold 2, then (1,0) threshold 3. Spread diagonally so dots distribute evenly.

flashcards.q: How do you apply simple dithering? What is the size penalty?
flashcards.a: Rescale pixel: ‚åäpixel √∑ 51.2‚åã for 2√ó2 (range 0‚Äì4). If rescaled > matrix_entry ‚Üí dot ON. Each pixel becomes an n√ón block, so the output image is n¬≤ times larger (2√ó2 = 4√ó, 4√ó4 = 16√ó).

flashcards.q: Walk through: simple dithering, pixel=180, 2√ó2 matrix [[0,2],[3,1]].
flashcards.a: Rescaled = ‚åä180/51.2‚åã = 3. Compare 3 against each entry: (0,0): 3>0 ON; (0,1): 3>2 ON; (1,0): 3>3 NO; (1,1): 3>1 ON. Result: 3/4 dots on.

flashcards.q: What is ordered dithering? How does it avoid the size penalty?
flashcards.a: Matrix tiles across the whole image. Each pixel at (i,j) uses threshold at (i mod n, j mod n). Rescale ‚åäpixel/64‚åã for 2√ó2. If rescaled > threshold ‚Üí black. Each output pixel = one input pixel ‚Üí same image size.

flashcards.q: In ordered dithering, pixel at (1,0)=100, matrix [[0,2],[3,1]], divisor=64. Output?
flashcards.a: Position: (1 mod 2, 0 mod 2) = (1,0). Threshold = 3. Rescaled = ‚åä100/64‚åã = 1. Is 1 > 3? NO ‚Üí output 0 (white).

problems.q: Simple dithering with 2√ó2 matrix $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ (divisor=51.2). Apply to pixel value 130. How many dots are ON? Which positions?
problems.a:
Rescaled = $\lfloor 130/51.2 \rfloor = 2$.

Compare 2 against each matrix entry:
- (0,0): 2 > 0? **YES ‚Üí ON**
- (0,1): 2 > 2? **NO ‚Üí OFF**
- (1,0): 2 > 3? **NO ‚Üí OFF**
- (1,1): 2 > 1? **YES ‚Üí ON**

**2 dots ON** ‚Äî positions (0,0) and (1,1) (diagonal pattern).
problems.hint: Rescale first (√∑51.2), then compare with each entry using strict >

problems.q: Ordered dithering with 2√ó2 matrix $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, divisor=64. Pixel at position $(3,2)$ has value 120. Output?
problems.a:
Matrix position: $(3 \bmod 2, 2 \bmod 2) = (1, 0)$. Threshold = **3**.

Rescaled = $\lfloor 120/64 \rfloor = 1$.

Is $1 > 3$? **NO ‚Üí output 0 (white)**.
problems.hint: (row mod 2, col mod 2) to find matrix position

problems.q: Ordered dithering, same matrix and divisor. Pixel at $(3,3)$ has value 250. Output?
problems.a:
Matrix position: $(3 \bmod 2, 3 \bmod 2) = (1, 1)$. Threshold = **1**.

Rescaled = $\lfloor 250/64 \rfloor = 3$.

Is $3 > 1$? **YES ‚Üí output 1 (black)**.
problems.hint: 250 is very bright ‚Äî should produce a black dot

problems.q: Why does ordered dithering avoid the size penalty of simple dithering?
problems.a:
In simple dithering, each input pixel is **replaced by an entire n√ón block**, so the output is n¬≤ times larger.

In ordered dithering, the matrix tiles across the image as a **repeating threshold map**. Each output pixel still corresponds to exactly one input pixel ‚Äî just with a different threshold based on its position. Image size is unchanged.
problems.hint: Think about what happens to each pixel in each method
